story: When the monarch grew tired of a first minister, he or she could be dismissed, or worse: Cromwell was executed and Clarendon driven into exile when they lost favour. Kings sometimes divided power equally between two or more ministers to prevent one minister from becoming too powerful. Late in Anne's reign, for example, the Tory ministers Harley and St John shared power.
q1: Cromwell was executed when he lost the king's favour. (true/false)
q2: Queen Anne's two ministers shared equal power. (true/false)

story: A tipping point in the evolution of the prime ministership came with the death of Anne in 1714 and the accession of George I to the throne. George spoke no English, spent much of his time at his home in Hanover, and had neither knowledge of, nor interest in, the details of English government. In these circumstances it was inevitable that the king's first minister would become the de facto head of the government.
q1: George I spoke fluent English. (true/false)
q2: George I spent most of his time in Hanover. (true/false)

story: By the late 20th century, the majority of the world's countries had a prime minister or equivalent minister, holding office under either a constitutional monarchy or a ceremonial president. The main exceptions to this system have been the United States and the presidential republics in Latin America modelled on the U.S. system, in which the president directly exercises executive authority.
q1: The president directly exercises executive authority in the United States. (true/false)
q2: Most countries in the late 20th century had a prime minister. (true/false)

story: Appointment of the prime minister of France requires no approval by the parliament either, but the parliament may force the resignation of the government. In these systems, it is possible for the president and the prime minister to be from different political parties if the legislature is controlled by a party different from that of the president. When it arises, such a state of affairs is usually referred to as (political) cohabitation.
q1: In France, the parliament can force the government's resignation. (true/false)
q2: The French president appoints the prime minister with mandatory parliamentary consent. (true/false)

story: Canada's constitution, being a 'mixed' or hybrid constitution (a constitution that is partly formally codified and partly uncodified) originally did not make any reference whatsoever to a prime minister, with her or his specific duties and method of appointment instead dictated by "convention". In the Constitution Act, 1982, passing reference to a "Prime Minister of Canada" is added, though only regarding the composition of conferences of federal and provincial first ministers.
q1: Canada's Constitution Act of 1982 explicitly defined the prime minister's powers in detail. (true/false)
q2: The Constitution Act, 1982 mentions the Prime Minister of Canada. (true/false)

story: Most prime ministers in parliamentary systems are not appointed for a specific term in office and in effect may remain in power through a number of elections and parliaments. For example, Margaret Thatcher was only ever appointed prime minister on one occasion, in 1979. She remained continuously in power until 1990, though she used the assembly of each House of Commons after a general election to reshuffle her cabinet.
q1: Margaret Thatcher served as prime minister for more than one decade. (true/false)
q2: Thatcher was appointed prime minister multiple times during her tenure. (true/false)

story: The latter in effect allows the government to appeal the opposition of parliament to the electorate. However, in many jurisdictions a head of state may refuse a parliamentary dissolution, requiring the resignation of the prime minister and his or her government. In most modern parliamentary systems, the prime minister is the person who decides when to request a parliamentary dissolution.
q1: In most modern parliamentary systems, the prime minister decides when to request parliamentary dissolution. (true/false)
q2: A head of state can prevent a prime minister from requesting parliamentary dissolution. (true/false)

story: In the Russian constitution the prime minister is actually titled Chairman of the government while the Irish prime minister is called the Taoiseach (which is rendered into English as prime minister), and in Israel he is Rosh HaMemshalah meaning "head of the government". In many cases, though commonly used, "prime minister" is not the official title of the office-holder; the Spanish prime minister is the President of the Government (Presidente del Gobierno).
q1: The Spanish prime minister holds the official title of President of the Government. (true/false)
q2: The Irish prime minister is officially called the Prime Minister. (true/false)

story: Other common forms include president of the council of ministers (for example in Italy, Presidente del Consiglio dei Ministri), President of the Executive Council, or Minister-President. In the Scandinavian countries the prime minister is called statsminister in the native languages (i.e. minister of state). In federations, the head of government of subnational entities such as provinces is most commonly known as the premier, chief minister, governor or minister-president.
q1: In Italy, the prime minister is titled 'President of the Council of Ministers'. (true/false)
q2: The Scandinavian term for prime minister is 'minister of state'. (true/false)

story: In the UK, where devolved government is in place, the leaders of the Scottish, Northern Irish and Welsh Governments are styled First Minister. In India, The Prime Minister is referred to as "Pradhan Mantri", meaning "prime minister". In Pakistan, the prime minister is referred to as "Wazir-e-Azam", meaning "Grand Vizier".
q1: In India, the prime minister is referred to as 'Pradhan Mantri'. (true/false)
q2: The heads of government in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland are styled 'Prime Ministers'. (true/false)

story: The Prime Minister's executive office is usually called the Office of the Prime Minister in the case of the Canada and other Commonwealth countries, it is called Cabinet Office in United Kingdom. Some Prime Minister's office do include the role of Cabinet. In other countries, it is called the Prime Minister's Department or the Department of the Prime Minister and Cabinet as for Australia.
q1: The Prime Minister's executive office in Canada is called the Department. (true/false)
q2: The Office of the Prime Minister is the name used in Canada and other Commonwealth countries. (true/false)

story: In the higher education sector, there are seven designated Universities of Technology in Australia (though, note, not all use the phrase "university of technology", such as the Universities of Canberra and South Australia, which used to be Colleges of Advanced Education before transitioning into fully-fledged universities with the ability - most important of all - to confer doctorates):
q1: There are seven designated Universities of Technology in Australia. (true/false)
q2: The Universities of Canberra and South Australia used to be research institutes. (true/false)

story: Fachhochschule is a German type of tertiary education institution and adopted later in Austria and Switzerland. They do not focus exclusively on technology, but may also offer courses in social science, medicine, business and design. They grant bachelor's degrees and master's degrees, and focus more on teaching than research and more on specific professions than on science.
q1: Fachhochschule institutions focus primarily on research. (true/false)
q2: Fachhochschule is only offered in Germany. (true/false)

story: In the Netherlands, four former institutes of technology have become universities over the past decades. These are the current three Technical Universities (at Delft, Eindhoven and Enschede), plus the former agricultural institute in Wageningen. A list of all hogescholen in the Netherlands, including some which might be called polytechnics, can be found here.
q1: There are currently three Technical Universities in the Netherlands. (true/false)
q2: The agricultural institute in Wageningen remained an institute and did not become a university. (true/false)

story: In Canada, there are Affiliate Schools, Colleges, Institutes of Technology/Polytechnic Institutes, and Universities that offer instruction in a variety of programs that can lead to: engineering and applied science degrees, apprenticeship and trade programs, certificates, and diplomas. Affiliate Schools are polytechnic divisions belonging to a national university and offer select technical and engineering programs. Colleges, Institutes of Technology/Polytechnic Institutes, and Universities tend to be independent institutions.
q1: Affiliate Schools in Canada belong to a national university. (true/false)
q2: All institutions in Canada offering engineering programs are independent. (true/false)

story: One of the oldest observatories in South America is the Quito Astronomical Observatory. Founded in 1873 and located 12 minutes south of the Equator in Quito, Ecuador. The Quito Astronomical Observatory is the National Observatory of Ecuador and is located in the Historic Center of Quito and is managed by the National Polytechnic School.
q1: The Quito Astronomical Observatory is located north of the Equator. (true/false)
q2: The facility is managed by the National Polytechnic School. (true/false)

story: University of Engineering & Technology or University of Engineering Sciences are the recognized universities that grant Bachelor's and master's degrees in undergraduate and graduate studies respectively. The Bachelor of Science degree awarded by Universities of Engineering & Technology or University of Engineering Sciences are 4 years full-time program after finishing 13 years of education (international high school certificate) in Pakistan known as F.Sc equivalent to British system A-Level.
q1: Pakistani engineering universities grant all degrees in 4-year programs. (true/false)
q2: F.Sc is equivalent to the American SAT examination. (true/false)

story: Beginning in 2004 former Technikons have either been merged with traditional Universities to form Comprehensive Universities or have become Universities of Technology, however the Universities of Technology have not to date acquired all of the traditional rights and privileges of a University (such as the ability to confer a wide range of degrees).
q1: Universities of Technology in South Africa can confer a wide range of degrees. (true/false)
q2: Technikons were either merged with universities or became Universities of Technology. (true/false)

story: Most of Thailand's institutes of technology were developed from technical colleges, in the past could not grant bachelor's degrees; today, however, they are university level institutions, some of which can grant degrees to the doctoral level. Examples are Pathumwan Institute of Technology (developed from Pathumwan Technical School), King Mongkut's Institute of Technology Ladkrabang (Nondhaburi Telecommunications Training Centre), and King Mongkut's Institute of Technology North Bangkok (Thai-German Technical School).
q1: Thai institutes of technology were originally technical colleges. (true/false)
q2: Thai technical colleges could confer bachelor's degrees in the past. (true/false)

story: The Wayback Machine is a digital archive of the World Wide Web and other information on the Internet created by the Internet Archive, a nonprofit organization, based in San Francisco, California, United States. It was set up by Brewster Kahle and Bruce Gilliat, and is maintained with content from Alexa Internet. The service enables users to see archived versions of web pages across time, which the archive calls a "three dimensional index."
q1: The Wayback Machine was set up by Brewster Kahle and Bruce Gilliat. (true/false)
q2: The Internet Archive is headquartered in Los Angeles, California. (true/false)
